text: The surgeon inspects the hernia site located three centimeters below the umbilicus, ensuring that the hernia contents consist only of omentum and confirming the absence of bowel involvement.
The surgeon uses monopolar energy to sharply dissect thick fibrous strands within the hernia site, ensuring that only omentum is present and no bowel is nearby.

The surgeon identifies and dissects thick fibrous strands using monopolar cautery, revealing underlying fat. Gentle manipulation is applied to reduce the omentum, avoiding firm tugging to prevent damage and bleeding. Counter pressure is applied externally, and the reduction proceeds in a clockwise manner. Remaining fibrous strands are divided sharply, completing the reduction of the omental content.
The surgeon inspects the hernia sac to ensure no remnant omentum is left behind, which could become gangrenous and cause complications. A small secondary defect is visible through the primary defect.

The surgeon makes a small incision in the preperitoneal layer, approximately seven centimeters above the apical portion of the defect, to raise a flap. The dissection begins to demonstrate a plane suitable for incisional hernia or primary umbilical or paraumbilical hernia repair.

The surgeon demonstrates the pre-peritoneal plane, which is commonly used for trans-abdominal pre-peritoneal repair of inguinal hernias. The video shows the surgeon entering a deeper plane, revealing a small buttonhole on the posterior rectus sheath. The surgeon then enters the retrorectus plane, where the mesh will be placed according to the Stopa technique.

The surgeon identifies the transversalis fascia and a small portion of the posterior rectus sheath posteriorly. They proceed to divide the peritoneum along with the sheath around the defect due to its fibrous nature, which prevented complete sac dissection. The surgeon enters the Stoppa plane below the defect and continues dissecting in the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon manipulates a pad of fat and the transversalis fascia, revealing the border of the rectus muscle. Small fibrous fatty structures are visible and are divided to open the plane quickly. The inferior epigastric artery is seen just above the surgical instrument as the surgeon enters the lateral portion beyond the rectus muscle.

The surgeon demonstrates the entire dissected area, noting minimal rectal diastasis below the hernia. A loop is brought through a small stab incision on the lower aspect of the defect, and a suture is placed on one edge of the defect, which measures approximately 3 by 2.5 centimeters, to perform a primary closure.

The surgeon takes bites on either side of the edge of the abdominal wall defect, manipulating the tissue to facilitate suturing. The sutures are placed to include only the linea alba, avoiding excessive thickness of the rectus abdominis muscle, aiming to bring the muscles together at the midline.

So in a country like India, where cost is always a factor, we find that trying to replicate the gold standard of incisional hernia repair, namely the Renastopa technique laparoscopically, is a very good alternative and also is a very cost-effective alternative. not only that it also negates all the negative things that can happen with the placement of a mesh intra-abdominally even though they do have the special protection in the form of a dual layer no mesh is proven to be the ideal mesh yet so all meshes are prone for complications whereas this technique would totally negate that as well as give the patient a very sound repair with a very low proven recurrence rate. The other utility of performing this dissection is that once we become very comfortable in establishing these planes and dissecting them then we can utilize the same planes for a tar release in cases of a large defect where a posterior component separation is required.

The surgeon retrieves one limb of the Vicryl suture attached to a 15x15 cm lightweight polypropylene mesh, which is visibly macroporous. Two central trans-facial sutures are placed in the midline to anchor the mesh anteriorly, using intracorporeal sutures. The remaining sutures are anchored with absorbable 2-0 Vicryl material, avoiding lateral trans-facial sutures to prevent nerve entrapment and chronic pain.

The surgeon takes bites at regular intervals to loosely suture the upper left corner of the mesh to the muscle using intracorporeal suturing. The mesh is neatly placed on the anterior abdominal wall, and the defect created by the dissection around the hernia is sutured with Vicryl.

The surgeon inspects the raised flap for buttonhole defects and finds none. The right side edge of the flap is closed using a running suture technique. The mesh covers a wide area, restoring the abdominal wall physiology effectively. The completed flap is intact without any defects.
